name: american chili
instructions:
The american chili — beans-forward, cheese-topped, served over spaghetti (cincinnati-adjacent tradition, or just because pasta works). Structurally the OPPOSITE of chile colorado in the cookbook: here beans are the star and meat is a supporting texture; chile colorado is the meat-forward dish where beans are optional.

BASIC INGREDIENTS (the non-negotiables):
- 1-2 cans black beans, pinto beans, and/or red beans (kidney) — mix and match, at least 3 cans total across bean types
- 1 can canned tomatoes
- 1 can corn
- 1 white onion, finely chopped
- 1 bell pepper, finely chopped (green traditional; red/orange/yellow fine)
- 4-6 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 bay leaf
- 1 tbsp cumin
- 1 tsp oregano
- new mexican chili powder + ancho chili powder (both — see notes)
- 1-2 cups chicken stock OR vegetable stock (see notes on choice)
- shredded cheddar cheese for serving
- cooked spaghetti for serving
- salt and black pepper

CONDIMENT STACK (adjust to taste — the more the better, see 'ANYTHING GOES' note):
- ketchup — a squeeze
- yellow mustard OR dijon mustard — a squeeze (both work; yellow is more traditional-american, dijon adds a sharper tang)
- worcestershire sauce — a splash

OPTIONAL MEAT (real or faux — beans are still the star):
- ground beef, ground turkey, impossible meat, or ground vegan meat — 1/2 to 1 lb

PROCEDURE:

1. FINELY CHOP THE AROMATICS BY KNIFE — NEVER FOOD PROCESSED: finely chop the onion, bell pepper, and garlic with a knife. Do NOT food-process or purée them — chunky texture is part of the american chili identity, and processing pushes the dish toward chile-colorado / smooth-sauce territory.

2. SAUTÉ THE AROMATIC BASE: heat oil in a large pot. Add finely chopped onion, bell pepper, garlic, and bay leaf. Cook until softened (5-8 min).

3. (OPTIONAL) BROWN THE MEAT: if using meat, push aromatics to the side and brown the ground meat in the same pot. Break it up as it cooks.

4. ADD TOMATO: stir in the canned tomatoes (and optional tomato paste). Cover and let steam / let the tomatoes dissolve, ~10 min. Break up the whole tomatoes with a wooden spoon as they soften.

5. ADD BEANS AND CORN: add all the drained beans and the drained corn. Stir to combine.

6. SEASON: cumin, oregano, new mexican chili powder, ancho chili powder, salt, and pepper. Ketchup, mustard, and worcestershire sauce all to taste — squeeze and splash generously.

7. ADD STOCK: pour in chicken or vegetable stock, enough to bring everything to a good chili consistency (not soupy, not dry — should be scoopable and coat the beans/meat).

8. SIMMER: 30-45 min on low, stirring occasionally. Longer is fine.

9. TASTE AND ADJUST: this is where 'anything goes' — see notes. More ketchup? more worcestershire? a splash of hot sauce? porcini powder? nutritional yeast? all valid.

10. SERVE OVER SPAGHETTI, TOPPED WITH SHREDDED CHEDDAR: ladle over cooked spaghetti (drained). Top generously with shredded cheddar (or another cheese — see notes).
notes:
This is one of the two big cookbook chilis. The other is chile-colorado (mexican dried-chile sauce dish). They are structurally OPPOSED and should not be conflated — see comparison below.

=== THE BASICS — WHAT'S NON-NEGOTIABLE ===

The basics that make this recognizable as american chili: beans (multiple types), canned tomatoes, chopped onions, chili powder, and shredded cheddar cheese on top. Everything else is optional, adjustable, and often improvable via weird additions.

=== 'ANYTHING GOES' BEYOND THE BASICS — THE MORE WEIRD, THE BETTER ===

American chili is a genuinely flexible dish. Beyond the basics above, additions are welcomed and often improvements. Some real ones I've done and endorse:
- Ketchup, mustard, worcestershire — the base condiment stack (all in primary above).
- Hot sauce — secondary, add to taste.
- Nutritional yeast — secondary, adds cheesy-umami depth.
- Porcini mushroom powder — secondary, adds meaty-woodsy depth.
- Other cheeses (see cheese note below) — parmesan, gouda, monterrey jack all work in addition to or instead of cheddar.
- A splash of coffee, a square of dark chocolate, a pinch of cinnamon, molasses, brown sugar — all in the traditional 'weird chili additions' tradition; small amounts add depth.
Don't be precious about the ingredient list. Chili benefits from a slightly chaotic condiment approach — it's the platonic 'kitchen sink' dish.

=== BEANS ARE THE STAR (UNLIKE CHILE COLORADO) ===

BEANS ARE THE STRUCTURAL AND FLAVOR HEADLINE HERE: at least 3 cans of beans across black + pinto + red (kidney). This is the defining feature vs chile-colorado, where the meat is the star and beans are optional (see chile-colorado for the contrasting stance). The mix of bean types gives visual and textural interest — black beans are firmer and darker, pinto softer and creamier, kidney the traditional chili shape. Use all three, or two of the three, but don't skimp on the total quantity.

MEAT IS OPTIONAL AND SUPPORTING: real ground beef, ground turkey, impossible meat, and ground vegan meat all work equally well here. Unlike chile colorado (which requires meat and specifically wants chunky beef or ground meat with beans as a secondary meat slot), american chili's meat is a texture and umami contributor to the beans-forward whole. 1/2 to 1 lb is plenty for a big pot; some versions skip meat entirely and rely on the beans + condiment umami stack.

=== CHOPPING RULES — FINELY BY KNIFE, NEVER PROCESSED ===

FINELY CHOPPED BUT NEVER FOOD-PROCESSED OR PUREED: the onions, bell pepper, and garlic all get finely CHOPPED by knife, not run through a food processor and not pureed. Chunky texture in the aromatic base is part of the american chili identity. Food-processed vegetables give you a smooth-sauce texture that's chile-colorado territory (specifically, the food-processor-dice base for the mexican dried-chile sauce). Two different dishes, two different textures — keep the knife-chop honest for american chili.

=== TOMATO — CANNED IS PRIMARY, PASTE IS SECONDARY ===

Canned tomatoes are the primary tomato source; optional tomato paste is fine as a depth booster (secondary). Cover the pot after adding tomatoes and let the whole tomatoes steam-dissolve into the base — you can help them along with a wooden spoon. Don't reach for fresh tomatoes here — canned is the right register for american chili, and fresh reads too bright/summery.

=== STOCK — CHICKEN OR VEGETABLE, BOTH WORK ===

Chicken stock is the more traditional and slightly richer choice; vegetable stock works fine and is the vegetarian default. Bouillon cubes are acceptable in a pinch (this isn't a delicate french dish where cubes fight the register) but real stock reads cleaner.

=== CHEESE — CHEDDAR IS DEFAULT BUT ANYTHING SHREDDED WORKS ===

Shredded cheddar is the classic american-chili topping. But other shredded cheeses all work and are worth experimenting with:
- PARMESAN: adds sharp umami and a slightly italian register alongside the spaghetti-and-chili base. Really good.
- GOUDA: mellow, slightly sweet, melts smoothly. Different profile from cheddar but excellent.
- MONTERREY JACK: neutral melting cheese, tips the dish more mexican-ish. Works.
All of these are worth trying. Cheddar is the default because it's what american chili tradition expects, but 'anything shredded' is a valid frame.

=== SERVED OVER SPAGHETTI ===

This recipe is served over spaghetti — cincinnati-chili-adjacent. The spaghetti gives the chili something to coat, catches the sauce, and rounds the meal out. If you'd rather serve without pasta, that's fine too (chili over rice, chili with cornbread, or just a bowl of chili with cheese are all valid), but the spaghetti pairing is worth trying if you haven't.

=== HOW THIS DIFFERS FROM CHILE COLORADO (SEE CHILE-COLORADO) ===

- STAR PROTEIN: beans are the star here; meat is the star in chile colorado.
- CHILE FORM: chili powder (new mexican + ancho) here; whole dried chiles (guajillo + ancho + pasilla + california + new mexican powder) in chile colorado. Rehydration and blending are chile colorado technique; not needed here.
- ONION TREATMENT: finely CHOPPED by knife here; food-processor-diced or sauté-then-blended in chile colorado.
- CONDIMENT STACK: ketchup + mustard + worcestershire are canonical here; chile colorado explicitly avoids all three (they push toward american-chili register, which is what chile colorado is trying to escape).
- TOMATO: canned tomatoes primary here (with optional paste); tomato paste ONLY in chile colorado (canned tomatoes fully clashing there).
- CUMIN: canonical here; also fine in chile colorado (both use it).
- CHEDDAR CHEESE: canonical topping here; secondary in chile colorado ('american-chili move' when leaning american-heartier).
- SPAGHETTI: served over here; not part of chile colorado.
- COOK TIME: 30-45 min simmer here; chile colorado is longer (especially the chunky-beef path).
The two dishes share some ingredients (beans, cumin, onions, chili powder) but they're structurally opposed. Don't try to hedge between them.
